did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning (specifically a proposed HIDADL model) and machine learning techniques for skin cancer classification. The methodology involves optimizing input images using an artificial immune optimization algorithm, extracting features (histogram and DWT), and training a deep learning model (convolutional neural network) on the HAM10000 dataset of dermoscopic skin lesion images to classify skin cancer classes.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'Research Paper' on the first page. It presents original experimental work, including a proposed HIDADL model, methodology sections detailing artificial immune optimization and deep learning, and results comparing precision, recall, and accuracy metrics on the HAM10000 dataset against a baseline model.

title: HEALTH DATA ANALYSIS FOR CANCER PREDICTION BY OPTIMIZING FEATURES & DEEP LEARNING MODEL
journal: Int. J. Engg. Res. & Sci. & Tech.
brief_summary: This study proposes the HIDADL model for skin cancer classification using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. The methodology involves optimizing input images via an artificial immune optimization algorithm, followed by feature extraction using histogram and Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT). These features are then used to train a deep learning convolutional neural network for classification. The model's performance was evaluated against the DeepSkin baseline using precision, recall, and accuracy metrics across varying dataset sizes. Results indicate that the proposed HIDADL model improved precision by 21.38% and accuracy by 10.57% compared to the DeepSkin model. The findings suggest that image optimization and feature extraction significantly enhance the detection accuracy of skin cancer classes.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies 'skin cancer' using the HAM10000 dataset which contains 'seven skin lesion categories'. The paper does not list the specific individual lesion types (e.g., melanoma, BCC) but refers to the target as 'skin cancer' or 'skin cancer image class'.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer image class (seven skin lesion categories)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes the HIDADL model to classify skin cancer images from the HAM10000 dataset into seven skin lesion categories. The model uses artificial immune optimization for image preprocessing, extracts histogram and DWT features, and trains a deep neural network (CNN) for classification. Performance is evaluated using precision, recall, accuracy, and f-measure metrics for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 10,015 dermoscopic images of skin lesions across seven skin lesion categories. The paper references this dataset in the Related Work section and uses it as the source for the 'real dataset images of skin cancer' used in the experiments, citing reference [20] (http://www.dermnet.com/dermatology-pictures-skin-disease-pictures).

use.training: Used for training the proposed HIDADL model and the baseline DeepSkin model. The study evaluated performance on varying subset sizes (40, 80, 120, 160, 200 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the models with varying dataset sizes (40, 80, 120, 160, 200 images) to evaluate precision, recall, accuracy, and f-measure.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed HIDADL model and the DeepSkin baseline were made using descriptive metrics (precision, recall, accuracy, f-measure) and percentage improvements without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.experiment: Comparison of HIDADL vs DeepSkin across varying dataset sizes (40, 80, 120, 160, 200 images)
HAM10000.notes: The HIDADL model increased precision by 21.38% and accuracy by 10.57% compared to the DeepSkin model. Recall values improved by 1.78%. Detection accuracy decreased with an increase in the number of testing images for both models.

architecture_used.main_model: HIDADL (Health Image Data Analysis using Deep Learning)

architecture_details.model_family: Deep Neural Network (DNN) / Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)
architecture_details.backbone: Not explicitly named; described as a DNN with convolutional and pooling layers applied iteratively
architecture_details.preprocessing: Artificial Immune Optimization (AIO) algorithm for image optimization; Histogram and Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) feature extraction (16 histogram values, 16 DWT low-frequency sub-band values)
architecture_details.layers: Convolutional and pooling layers applied iteratively
architecture_details.classifier_head: Deep Neural Network (DNN) for classification of tumor vs non-tumor regions

training_details.hardware: 4GB RAM, Windows operating system, I3 processor

classification_or_output_head.output_description: Classifies optimized image blocks as tumor or non-tumor

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: HIDADL (Health Image Data Analysis using Deep Learning)
preprocessing_techniques: Artificial Immune Optimization (AIO) algorithm for image optimization/enhancement
Conversion of input image to a two-dimensional matrix with pixel intensity values ranging between 0 and 255
Reshaping of the image to facilitate matrix computations
Filtering process applied to suppress noise and eliminate unwanted data

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for image pre-processing and optimization to enhance input images by suppressing noise and eliminating unwanted data, rather than traditional data augmentation for increasing dataset variability. The algorithm generates antibodies (potential solutions), evaluates affinity (fitness), and updates the population to find the best pixel cluster center set.
A filtering process was applied to the input images to suppress noise and eliminate unwanted data during the pre-processing stage.

conclusions: The proposed HIDADL model, which combines artificial immune optimization for image preprocessing with histogram and Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) feature extraction, improves skin cancer classification accuracy compared to the DeepSkin baseline.
The HIDADL model increased precision by 21.38% and correct class detection accuracy by 10.57% compared to the DeepSkin model.
The use of the artificial immune genetic algorithm enhanced input data quality by optimizing the input images.
The extraction of visual color histogram features reduced learning complexity and improved work performance.
The deep learning convolutional neural network component of the model increased the detection class capability.
Detection accuracy for both the proposed HIDADL and DeepSkin models decreased as the number of testing images increased.
The proposed model improved recall values by 1.78% compared to the baseline.
The study was conducted on a real dataset of skin cancer images (HAM10000) using a system with 4GB RAM, Windows OS, and an I3 processor.
important_remarks: The study evaluates model performance on extremely small, non-standard dataset subsets (40, 80, 120, 160, 200 images) rather than the full HAM10000 dataset or standard train/test splits, limiting the generalizability of the results.

The paper lacks statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the reported performance improvements (21.38% precision increase, 10.57% accuracy increase) over the DeepSkin baseline.
The experimental setup was conducted on low-spec hardware (4GB RAM, Windows OS, I3 processor), which may not reflect the computational requirements for deploying such models in clinical settings.

The methodology relies on 'Artificial Immune Optimization' for image preprocessing, which is a non-standard technique compared to typical data augmentation or normalization methods used in deep learning for medical imaging.

The paper does not report per-class performance metrics, making it impossible to assess the model's ability to distinguish between specific skin lesion types (e.g., melanoma vs. benign lesions) within the seven categories.
The baseline comparison is limited to a single prior model (DeepSkin), lacking comparison with state-of-the-art architectures or recent benchmarks in skin cancer classification.
The dataset source is cited as a generic URL (dermnet.com) rather than the official HAM10000 repository, raising potential concerns about data provenance and version control.
